doc.title: Issue bonds
doc.event: A company issues term bonds on an interest date. The market rate at issuance — not the coupon printed on the bond — decides what cash comes in: equal to face (par), more than face (premium), or less than face (discount).

variants.label: Market rate ABOVE the coupon — investors pay less than face

lines.label: PV at the market rate
lines.why: Cash received IS the issue price: the present value of every future coupon and the face, discounted at the MARKET rate. Investors will not pay face for a coupon that underpays the market.
lines.trap: Cash is never just 'face' when the rates differ — the market rate reprices the whole bond on day one.

lines.label: face − issue price
lines.why: The shortfall between face and cash received sits in a contra-liability. It is extra interest cost you will recognize over the term as it amortizes away.
lines.trap: Discount starts with D and increases with a DEBIT — it is contra to Bonds Payable, not an expense on day one.

lines.label: face
lines.why: Bonds Payable is always recorded at FACE — the amount contractually due at maturity — no matter what cash came in.
lines.trap: Never credit Bonds Payable for the issue price. Face goes here; the difference lives in the discount or premium account.

computationPaths.narration: Issue price = PV of the coupon annuity + PV of the face, both at the market periodic rate. Stated < market, so the PV lands below face — the gap is the discount.

steps.label: Issue price (cash received)
steps.formulaText: PV of coupons + PV of face @ market rate

variants.label: Market rate BELOW the coupon — investors pay more than face

lines.label: PV at the market rate
lines.why: The coupon beats the market, so investors bid the price above face. Cash received is still just the PV at the market rate.
lines.trap: A premium is not a gain — it is a reduction of future interest cost, recognized over the term.

lines.label: face
lines.why: Face at maturity is the contract; Bonds Payable carries face.
lines.trap: Never record Bonds Payable at the issue price.

lines.label: issue price − face
lines.why: The excess over face is an adjunct liability — it will amortize DOWN and shave every period's interest expense below the cash coupon.
lines.trap: Premium is credited at issuance (it grows the carrying value above face) — flipping it is the classic direction error.

computationPaths.narration: Same PV machine, opposite result: stated > market pushes the PV above face. The excess is the premium.

steps.label: Issue price (cash received)
steps.formulaText: PV of coupons + PV of face @ market rate

lines.label: = face
lines.why: Stated = market, so the PV of the bond IS face. Cash in equals face.
lines.trap: No discount or premium account belongs anywhere in a par issuance.

lines.label: face
lines.trap: Two-line entry. Adding a third line here means you've invented a discount or premium that doesn't exist.

memorize.body: Issue price = PV of coupon annuity + PV of face, BOTH discounted at the MARKET rate. The stated rate only sets the coupon amount inside that PV.
Rate story in one line: stated < market → discount (investors pay less); stated > market → premium; equal → par. The market rate always wins the pricing argument.
Balance-sheet presentation from day one: Bonds Payable at face, LESS discount (or PLUS premium) = carrying value. Exams love asking for the net number.

questions.prompt: Bonds are issued when the market rate is above the stated rate. How much cash does the issuer receive?

distractors.feedback: Face is what's repaid at maturity, not what comes in today. With market above stated, the bonds price BELOW face — cash received is the discounted PV.
That's one semiannual coupon, not the issue proceeds.

build.scaffold: Start from cash: it always equals the issue price (PV at the market rate). Bonds Payable always takes face. Whatever gap remains is your discount (debit) or premium (credit).
